text: The robotic setup includes Maryland forceps with bipolar energy, Cadea forceps, and a thoracic grasper to position the lung. The surgeon begins the right upper lobectomy by dissecting the station seven lymph node along the bronchus intermedius, addressing a small bronchial artery to open the subcarinal space. The Maryland bipolar forceps are used to peel the lymph node off the bronchus intermedius, aiming to remove it whole to minimize bleeding. The surgeon lifts the lymph node off the pericardium, moving towards the oesophagus, which is visible to the left, and peels the final parts from the oesophagus while approaching the carina, encountering small vessels from these structures.

The surgeon uses bipolar Maryland forceps to address adhesions to the chest wall, ensuring no evidence of invasion. The bronchus intermedius is visible, leading to the right upper lobe bronchus, with the azygos vein seen in the distance. The station R-ten hyaluronate lymph node is dissected whole to facilitate passing a stapler around the right upper lobe, reducing bleeding and ensuring a complete lymphadenectomy. The right upper lobe is positioned above the right upper lobe bronchus, with the bronchus intermedius below and the right main bronchus above, passing under the azygos to join the left main bronchus and form the trachea.

The surgeon uses the Cefolio method for port placement, inserting a 5mm port posteriorly, two 8mm ports in the eighth intercostal space with the camera centrally, and the assistant port in the ninth or tenth intercostal space anteriorly. The dissection focuses on the station R-eleven lymph node located between the right upper lobe and the bronchus intermedius. The Maryland forceps are used carefully to remove the lymph node, revealing the pulmonary artery to the right of the node. This dissection is crucial for visualizing the pulmonary artery and facilitating access around the right upper lobe bronchus.

The surgeon identifies the station R-Eleven S lymph node, located interlobar between the upper, lower, and middle lobes of the lung. This node is positioned superiorly, with its inferior counterpart situated between the middle and lower lobes.

The surgeon ensures complete removal of the lymph node before moving to the fissure between the upper and middle lobes. Adhesions are visible, but the fissure is well-defined, allowing clear visualization of the pulmonary artery. The upper lobe is retracted downward, revealing the azygos vein above, the right main bronchus, and the superior vena cava anteriorly.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy of station R-II and R-IV lymph nodes above the ozygous vein. The posterior limits of the vagus nerve are visible, and the station R-IV lymph node is carefully excised from beneath the ozygous vein. Anteriorly, the superior vena cava (SVC) is visible. The procedure aims to expose the entire trachea and the vagus nerve posteriorly, ensuring no fatty tissue remains for a complete lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon retracts the upper lobe posteriorly to identify the upper lobe vein. Care is taken to avoid damaging the pulmonary artery located behind the cadia. A Tan-Forty-Five stapler with a leader cath is prepared to negotiate the vessels, providing guidance for the assistant.

The surgeon rotates the Signia powered stapler to align with the vein and fires it smoothly. The right upper lobe bronchus is clearly visible next to the bronchus intermedius, aided by the removal of station XI and X lymph nodes. The pulmonary artery is also clearly seen. A purple 45 gold tip tri-stapler is used, and the bronchus is divided. Lymph nodes behind the bronchus are being removed to uncover the truncal branch.

The surgeon has moved to the anterior aspect to expose the pulmonary artery, revealing two truncal branches: anterior and apical. With the bronchus already taken, the area behind is clear, allowing for safe vessel firing using a Tan-Forty-Five stapler guided by a leader cath. The assistant operates from a low and anterior port, facilitating a straightforward approach.

The surgeon completes the pulmonary lobectomy by addressing the fissure between the upper and middle lobes. A Purple Sixty stapler is used to create straight staple lines, minimizing air leaks. The final firing completes the fissure, and the lobe is removed using an EWP bag through the assistant port.
